It is a small world
She said she loved Norway.
He told me he had cycled the coast.
She said she would call me.
He asked where I was from.
He said he lived in Bergen.
She said she had seen the fjords.
He said he would call me.
She asked if I liked it there.

You know, I keep running into you at these refuges, it really is a small world. I was just swapping stories with a couple from Chile, and the husband told me he had cycled the whole coast of Norway alone.
No way. What a story. Did he say how long it had taken him?
He said it had taken him two months, and he warned me the wind on the coast was brutal. He insisted the north was the most beautiful part, though.
Funny you bring that up. A fellow traveler in Albania once raved about the north of Norway. She told me I had to see the fjords before I got too old, and I reckon she was right.
So here you are. See, that chance encounter changed your plans. What else did she tell you?
She asked me why I kept putting the trip off, and I did not have a good answer. So I promised myself I would finally come. We still keep in touch, actually.
He told me he had lived there for years.
She said she would send me the photos.
He asked if I was travelling alone.
She warned me not to hike alone.
It really is a small world.

Hi Marcelo, it is Marit. I had to tell you this before I forgot. After you left the refuge, I got talking to that older gentleman by the fire, the one from Bergen. What a character. He told me he had been a fisherman for forty years, and he said the sea had taught him everything he knew. I asked him if he ever got frightened out there, and he just laughed. He said fear was normal, but that you learned to work with it. Then he brought up his grandson, who is learning English, and he insisted I write down some advice for the boy. So here it is, and it made me think of you. He said the ones who ask questions are the ones who keep learning. I thought that was rather beautiful. Anyway, safe travels, and do keep in touch. Bye now.

Welcome back to the show. Today, the traveler's tale, and why the best souvenir you bring home is a story someone told you. Here is something you notice after years on the road. You forget the hotels and the timetables, but you remember the conversations. A stranger on a train who told you her whole life story. A guide who warned you never to trust the weather in the mountains. A fellow traveler who insisted you change your entire route, and was completely right. And here is the interesting part. When you get home and retell those stories, the grammar changes. You stop saying she says, and you start saying she told me she had never been so happy. You step back in time. That little shift, from what people say to what they said, is how a moment becomes a memory. So collect those conversations. Ask people their stories. Because one day, retelling them is how you will travel all over again, without ever leaving your chair.
